text: The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar approximately fifteen centimeters below the rib edge, along with three eight millimeter trocars and a five millimeter incision for a Nathanson retractor to elevate the liver, creating space for the robotic sleeve gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon places a twelve millimeter trocar approximately fifteen centimeters below the rib edge, followed by three eight millimeter trocars and a five millimeter incision for a Nathanson retractor, which is used to elevate the liver and provide space for the operation.
The video shows a sped-up sequence where the surgeon identifies the anatomical structures and proceeds to separate the blood supply from the greater curvature of the stomach.
The video shows the surgeon identifying the anatomical structures and separating the blood supply from the greater curvature of the stomach, a crucial step in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to isolate, cut, and separate the blood vessels around the stomach, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The robotic vessel sealer is used to isolate, cut, and separate the vessels around the stomach, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon advances towards the spleen, approaching the angle of His, with visible anatomical landmarks guiding the progression.
The surgical team carefully advances towards the spleen and the angle of His, ensuring that only necessary tissue is manipulated during the procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies a hiatal hernia measuring approximately four centimeters in diameter.

The surgeon separates and visualizes the right and left crura of the diaphragm, ensuring the hiatus is appropriately tight to maintain the anti-reflux mechanism. The inferior vena cava is visible just to the left of the screen beneath the liver.
The surgeon separates and visualizes the right and left crura of the diaphragm, with the inferior vena cava visible just to the left of the screen beneath the liver.
The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection to separate the esophagus from adhesions connected to the diaphragmatic crura.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to carefully separate the esophagus from adhesions connected to the diaphragmatic crura.
The surgeon has placed a bougie into the esophagus of the patient, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon places a 36 French bougie into the esophagus of the patient, ensuring proper sizing and positioning for the procedure.

The surgeon uses a suction bougie, known as a visigi bougie, to calibrate the stapling during the sleeve gastrectomy. The bougie applies a slight vacuum to the intragastric space, ensuring clear visualization of the staple line and facilitating manipulation of the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a visigi bougie, a suction device, to calibrate the stapling during sleeve gastrectomy. The bougie applies a slight vacuum to the intragastric space, ensuring clear visualization of the staple line and esophagus manipulation.
The surgeon has identified the right and left crura and begins the repair of the diaphragmatic hernia, focusing on the anatomical structures visible beneath the liver.
The surgeon identifies the right and left crura of the diaphragm and begins the repair of the diaphragmatic hernia, focusing on the anatomical structures visible beneath the liver.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical structures, focusing on preserving the vagal nerves. Using a robotic system, they gently approximate the tissues with a number one Ethabon suture.
The surgeon uses a number one Ethabon suture with robotic assistance to gently approximate the tissue, ensuring precise alignment during the hernia repair.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to approximate the crural fibers, employing slip knots to aid in the diaphragmatic hernia repair. The robotic system provides enhanced visual control, compensating for the lack of tactile feedback.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to re-approximate the crural fibers with sutures, carefully closing the tissue with slip knots. The robotic tools provide precise control, aiding in the repair of the diaphragmatic hernia.
The surgeon uses sutures to re-approximate the tissue, achieving a precise repair of the diaphragmatic hernia with robotic assistance.
The surgeon uses three or four sutures to re-approximate the repair, placing them approximately seven to eight millimeters apart to avoid excessive tension and prevent tearing of the acryl fibers.
The surgeon uses the vessel sealer's cutting function instead of robotic scissors to minimize instrument usage, focusing on cost savings and procedural efficiency.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer with an integrated cutting function to perform precise tissue cutting during the procedure.
The surgeon tightens sutures using a large needle driver, ensuring an adequate repair, and then cuts the sutures with a vessel sealer.

The surgeon uses a large needle driver to tighten sutures, achieving an adequate repair. The vessel sealer is then used to cut the sutures, demonstrating the ability to pull the suture out correctly to avoid extra instrument passes.
The surgeon has completed the closure of the hiatus, indicating the end of the suturing process in the surgical field.
The surgeon has closed the hiatus, ensuring the repair is not overly tight and the left and right crura are well approximated. The stapling process is about to begin.
The surgeon assesses the approximation of the left and right crura, ensuring the sutures are not too tight, with visible alignment of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon initiates the stapling process, using a surgical stapler to secure tissue layers following the completed suturing of the hiatus.
The surgeon inspects the surgical area for any excess fat or herniated omentum that could interfere with the stapling process, ensuring a clear field for the procedure.

The surgeon inspects the surgical area for excess fat or herniated omentum that could interfere with the stapling process. The stomach is visually assessed for healthy color and viability before initiating the stapling procedure.
The surgeon inspects the stomach, assessing its color to ensure it appears healthy and viable.
The surgeon initiates the stapling process on the stomach, ensuring the tissue is healthy and viable, with no excess fat or herniated omentum interfering with the procedure.
The bougie is positioned within the stomach to calibrate the stapling process, ensuring accurate alignment and sizing during the procedure.
The surgeon positions the bougie to calibrate the stapling process, beginning approximately four centimeters from the pylorus using a robotic green stapler.
The surgeon positions the stapler approximately four centimeters from the pylorus to initiate the staple line on the stomach tissue.
The surgeon begins the stapling process using a robotic green stapler, ensuring precise alignment along the stomach tissue.
The surgeon uses the SureForm stapling device, which provides feedback cues to ensure the chosen staple is appropriate for the sensed tissue.

The surgeon aligns the staples along the stomach, ensuring a secure but not excessive bite of tissue. The staple line appears hemostatic, and the stapler's angulation allows for easy manipulation as the surgeon marks up towards the angle of His.

The surgeon aligns the staples along the stomach, ensuring a proper tissue bite without excess. The staple line appears hemostatic. The stapler's angulation aids in maneuvering as the surgeon flattens the stomach towards the angle of His.
The surgeon positions the stapler flat against the tissue, preparing for staple deployment.
The surgeon ensures a tension-free staple line at the angle of His, a critical area where leaks are most likely to occur, while firing the stapler.
The surgeon fires the stapler, ensuring a tension-free staple line along the stomach tissue.

The surgeon ensures that the gastric sleeve procedure results in a well-uniform cylindrical shape of the stomach, avoiding excessive tension or irregularities in the staple line. Care is taken not to leave too much of the fundus and to maintain a healthy amount of tissue while keeping the staple line smooth.
The surgeon inspects the remaining portion of the stomach for signs of bleeding and assesses its viability, ensuring it forms a healthy cylindrical tube without torsion.
The surgeon cuts the tip of a nasogastric (NG) tube and places it intra-abdominally, using the modified tube as a suction device during the procedure.

The surgeon cuts the tip of an NG tube and places it intra-abdominally to use as a suction device, aiming to reduce costs. A special fibrin glue is applied to control hemostasis, and a portion of omentum is positioned over the stomach to maintain a smooth angle and prevent torsion.
The surgeon applies a fibrin hemostatic agent to the surgical site, using the fiber and glue to control bleeding and ensure hemostasis.
The surgeon places a portion of the omentum over the stomach, visually covering the gastric surface with the fatty tissue.
The surgeon removes the stomach through a twelve millimeter incision and closes the incision using a Carter Thompson device.
The surgeon removes the stomach through a final twelve millimeter incision and closes the incision using a Carter Thompson device.
